title: Sequential Audit Sampling with Statistical Guarantees
summary: This paper develops a statistical framework for sequential audit sampling where auditors collect additional samples until sufficient evidence is obtained. The authors formulate the problem as sequential testing for a finite population under sampling without replacement, with exact boundary conditions calibrated via Monte Carlo simulation. The method applies to attribute auditing, deviation-rate auditing, and tests of controls.
key_points: Authors: Masahiro Kato and Kei Nakagawa, published April 7, 2026 on arXiv (paper ID 2604.06116, category q-fin.ST)
The paper addresses a gap in audit standards by providing statistical design for sequential audit procedures with additional sampling
Null and alternative hypotheses are defined using a tolerable deviation rate
Stopping and decision rules are formulated with exact sequential boundary conditions based on finite-population error probabilities
Monte Carlo simulation is used to calibrate boundaries at least-favorable deviation rates for practical implementation
The framework applies to attribute auditing, deviation-rate auditing, tests of controls, and can be extended to one-sided, two-stage, and truncated designs
why_it_matters: This is a methodological contribution to audit science, not a market or macro analysis. It provides auditors with a statistically rigorous framework for deciding when to stop sampling during financial statement audits. The paper has no direct relevance to market structure or macro catalysts and would not be useful for finance research focused on trading, liquidity, or economic policy.

Not suitable for a finance research archive focused on market structure and macro catalysts unless such an archive has a broader scope.
